summary: Patrick recounts how his Vista laptop's hard drive began failing, and how Dropbox saved his important files despite his procrastination on backups. He had signed up for Dropbox casually after a coworker mentioned it but hadn't seriously used it for backups. After fellow software developers suffered hard drive failures, he at least started backing up his servers, but his laptop remained unprotected. When the inevitable crash came, he managed to boot into safe mode and stream his critical files to Dropbox just in time.
key_points: Procrastination on backups is universal even among technically sophisticated people who know better
Having critical business data distributed across cloud services (Google, Slicehost, PayPal) provided natural redundancy even before a formal backup strategy
Dropbox's symlink trick on Vista allowed syncing files outside the Dropbox folder without risky copy operations on a failing drive
The post serves as a real-time testimonial for Dropbox's value proposition during an actual emergency
money_quotes: "I'm going to start a backup any day now" ranks right up there with "Hmm, well, we haven't heard from that freaky serial killer in a while. I think I'll stay in the house."

Most of my critical business data is already off of my laptop — its at a Google server farm, or chilling on a Slicehost server, or over at Paypal, but there is still quite a bit of sentimental value in my photos.
Today the inevitable happened: my Vista-using laptop decided to start the sputter and die deathspiral.

I think the hard disk covering some of the code loaded by all the bells&whistles that autoload when the machine boots is toast. Luckily untoasted is my ability to read the rest of the disk and run Dropbox.
key_insight: Even technically savvy people procrastinate on backups, making frictionless cloud sync tools like Dropbox valuable precisely because they work passively before disaster strikes.
